coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and formation mechanism of s-doped carbon dots from low-molecule-weight organics
coredata.dc:creator: Dai, Bin
Wu, Chengxiao
Lu, Yixuan
Deng, Degang
Xu, Shiqing
coredata.dc:description: The reaction temperature was controlled to investigate the formation process of S-doped CDs (CDs-S). Different low-molecule-weight organics were used as precursors, it reveals that the N and/or S atoms containing in the precursors are related to the formation and luminescence mechanism.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Low-molecule-weight organics
Formation mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of CDs-S prepared from thioacetic acid. Inset: corresponding particle size distribution histograms and SADE pattern of CDs-S. (c) XPS spectrum of CDs-S, (d) C1s spectrum, (e) N1s spectrum, (f) FTIR spectrum of CDs-S.
UV–vis absorption spectrum and PL spectra of the CDs-S prepared from CH3COSH. Insets: photographs of the aqueous solution of the CDs-S under daylight (left) and 365nm irradiation (right).
TEM images of the products at different reaction temperatures: the temperature reached 160°C (a), 180°C (b), 200°C (c), and 220°C (d).
(a) UV–vis absorption and (b) PL spectra of the CDs-S at different reaction temperatures.
A proposed formation mechanism of the CDs-S.
TEM images of CDs-O (a); CDs-N (b); CDs-N, S (c); and CDs-S (d).
TEM image of CDs-OH prepared from CH3CH2OH.
UV–Vis (a) and emission (λex=360nm) (b) spectra of CDs-O, CDs-N, CDs-N, S, CDs-S. Inset: the UV–Vis spectra of CDs-O and CDs-N.
UV–Vis spectra and emission spectra of CDs-O (a); CDs-N (b); CDs-N, S (c); CDs-S (d).
Fluorescence decay profile of the CDs-O, CDs-N, CDs-N, S, CDs-S monitored at 370nm.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and formation mechanism of s-doped carbon dots from low-molecule-weight organics

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, China Jiliang University, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering, China Jiliang University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The reaction temperature was controlled to investigate the formation process of S-doped CDs (CDs-S). Different low-molecule-weight organics were used as precursors, it reveals that the N and/or S atoms containing in the precursors are related to the formation and luminescence mechanism.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last decade, various researches focus on the synthesis and photoluminescence (PL) mechanism of carbon dots (CDs), which possess exceptional advantages such as excellent optical properties, biocompatibility, low toxicity and chemical stability [1,2], and these alluring properties make them promising candidates for plentiful applications, such as bioimaging [3,4], catalysis [5,6], and optical devices [7–9]. CDs, as the new member in the versatile carbon nanomaterial family, are generally small oxygenous carbon nanoparticles which are composed of sp [2] carbon atoms hybridized with abundant oxygenous residues, arising small conjugated carbon clusters (aromatic structures) embedded in oxygenous groups [10]. The isolated aromatic structures [11] possess suitable band gaps, and radiative recombination would generate visible fluorescence of the CDs. Thus, the precursors such as citric acids [12,13], glucose [14], egg [15], chitosan and alginate [16], orange juice [17],which with abundant carbon clusters and oxygenous group are generally used for the formation of CDs. Additionally, to enhance the optical properties, multifarious reagent have been doped to the CDs such as Al/N [12], S/N [18], Mg/N [19], etc.

However, quite few reports cared about whether the low-molecule-weight organics which just contain double C atoms such as acetic acid can be used as the precursors. And almost all of the previous reports synthesized the doped CDs through adding another reagent. It would become quit farfetched to illustrate the synthesis of CDs from low-molecule-weight if we doped anything else into. Thus, it is meaningful to explore a new process for the synthesis of CDs using a kind of low-molecule-weight with double C atoms as the carbon source and the dopants simultaneously.

How the CDs generate from low-molecular-weight organics is another important issue. A few researches have been reported about the formation mechanism of CDs from molecular precursors. Wang and co-workers reported that the CDs synthesized from ascorbic acid through polymerization and carbonization steps, which was similar to chemical reactions of glucose under hydrothermal conditions [20]. Giannelis group proposed a potential formation mechanism of CDs from pyrolysis of citric acid-ethanolamine precursor at different temperatures. The carbogenic core can start forming when the temperature is high enough and the PL is due to the presence of both molecular fluorophores and the carbogenic core [21]. Yu and co-workers propose a formation mechanism of the N-C-dots prepared from citric acid in MEA system, including polymerization, aromatization, nucleation, and growth [22].

In our work, distinguish with previous reports, for example, which used citric acid and l-cysteine as the carbon source and N/S source, respectively [23], or other disparate reagents that used as carbon and N/S source [18,24], we explore a facile strategy for the synthesis of sulfur single-doped CDs (CDs-S) from thioacetic acid which was used as the carbon and sulfur source simultaneously. The CDs-S exhibits excellent optical properties and homogeneous morphology. To investigate the formation process of CDs-S, we controlled the reaction temperature from 160°C to 220°C. It can be observed that the CDs-S generate consecutively through polymerization, nucleation and growth from the transmission electron microscopy (TEM). To further confirm the successful synthesis of CDs from low-molecule-weight organics, another four precursors were treated in the same procedure. The results reveal that low-molecule-weight organics (eg. acetic acid and alcohol) can’t be synthesized to CDs at 220°C without N and/or S atom on the precursors. Thus, it can be deduced that the N and/or S atom can promote the formation of CDs. Interestingly, the emission spectra of CDs from different precursors were consistent with the N and S states.

Thioacetamide (CH3CSNH2) (AR, ≥99wt%), thioacetic acid (CH3COSH) (AR, 95wt%), acetamide (CH3CONH2) (AR, 99wt%), were from Aladdin Company (Shanghai, China). Ethanol (CH3CH2OH) (AR, ≥99.7wt%) was from Hangzhou Gaojing Fine Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. Acetic acid (CH3COOH) (AR, ≥99.5wt%) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All the reagents were used as received without further purification. Deionized (DI) water was used in experiments.

Typically, 0.25mol of CH3COOH, CH3CH2OH, CH3CONH2, CH3COSH, and CH3CSNH2 were well dissolved in 80mL DI water, then the solution was transferred into a 100mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, heated at a constant temperature of 220°C for 10h, then the products centrifugalize at 12000r/min for 15min before dialysis for 24h (MWCO=3.5kD). The products were labelled as CDs-O, CDs-OH, CDs-N, CDs-S, CDs-N,S, respectively. Simultaneously, to further research the formation mechanism of CDs, CH3COSH were heated in the temperature for 200°C, 180°C, 160°C, 140°C in the same procedure.

XPS spectra were measured on a PHI 5000 Versa Probe X-ray photoelectron spectrometer using Al Kα as the source. The Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectrum were measured on FTIR (BrokTensor27). The photoluminescence spectrum was analyzed with PL3-211-P spectrometer (HORIBA JOBIN YVON, America) at room temperature excited by a 450W xenon lamp. The UV–Vis spectrum was obtained by Shimadzu UV-3600 UV–Vis spectrometer. The morphology and microstructure of the CNPs were examined with a HitachiH-9500 transmission electron microscope (TEM).

The morphology was characterized by TEM. As shown in TEM images ( Fig. 1a), CDs-S, which exhibit quasi-sphere in morphology, are well dispersed and the corresponding particle size distribution is between 3.8–7.4nm. From the Fig. 1b it can be observed that the lattice spacing of 0.26nm showing in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) corresponds to the (100) in-plane of graphite [25].

The composition of the CDs-S can be detected by XPS. The full spectrum (Fig. 1c) exhibits strong signals of O1s at 531eV, C1s at 285eV, and S2p at 164eV, with atomic percentages of 30.33%, 61.68% and 7.99%. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 1d) reveals three peaks at 284.5, 285.5 and 288.0eV, which are attributed to C-C, C-S/C-O, and C˭O bond, respectively [23]. The S2p spectrum (Fig. 1e) can be fitted into three components at 163.7, 164.9, and 168.2eV, which reveals the presence of -C-S- for S2p1/2, S2p3/2 and C-SO [24,26]. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1f) was also measured to characterize the structure and composition of CDs-S. It can be observed that there is an obvious peak at 3430cm−1 in the spectra of CDs-S, which should be ascribed to the O-H stretching vibration. The peak at 2510cm−1 was attributed to the -SH vibrations and the broad peaks from 980 to 1200cm−1 were due to the stretching vibrations of C-O-C and C-S-C bond, which indicated the sulfur have been doped to the CDs-S. The peak at 1656cm−1 assigned to the C˭O stretching in the conjugated structure. A weak peak at 620cm−1 can also be observed due to the bending vibration of the C-S bond, which confirmed the doping of sulfur element.

To investigate the optical properties of the CDs-S, absorption and PL spectra were studied ( Fig. 2). The CDs-S exhibit two absorption peaks at 276nm and 320nm, which due to the n-π* transition of C˭O bond and the surface states [23,27], respectively. The PL spectra indicate the excitation-dependent PL property of the CDs-S, in which the PL peak red-shifts from 455 to 525nm with the excitation wavelength changed from 360 to 480nm. This PL phenomenon is probably due to the variation of nanoparticle sizes and the surface states. The strongest fluorescence emission with full width at half-maximum as about 100nm is observed centered at 460nm under 380nm excitation. As shown in the inset of Fig. 2, the CDs-S solution was blue under the UV light irradiation (365nm).

To gain insight into the formation process of the CDs-S, the products at different reaction temperatures were taken out for TEM characterization. As shown in the TEM images ( Fig. 3), the morphology of CDs-S changed with the increase of temperature. Before the hydrothermal treated, the precursor was dissolved in water and stirred at room temperature. No nanoparticles and polymer were found by TEM at this stage. When the thioacetic acid was treated by 160°C, polymers with irregular pattern were obtained (Fig. 3a). Then, the polymer shrank through dehydration, and particles were gradually emerged with the scattering of polymer as the temperature increased to 180°C (Fig. 3b). As the temperature comes to 200°C, with the further shrunken polymeric fragments, the nanoparticles were dispersed batter that the a few CDs-S were found with diameter of about 10nm (Fig. 3c). Raising the temperature to 220°C, the polymeric fragments disappeared and the particle diameter of the CDs-S was about 6nm (Fig. 3d) which has been discussed before.

The UV–vis absorption and PL spectra are consistent with the TEM results. As shown in the UV–vis absorption spectra ( Fig. 4a), at 160°C, there was a broad absorbance below 400nm without any obvious peak. Heating to 180°C, although the CDs-S solution shows a broad UV/Vis absorption below 400nm, an absorption peak at 300nm assigned to n-π* appeared, indicating the formation of carbon nanoparticles. At 200°C, the absorption peak enhanced. When the temperature comes to 220°C, the peak enhanced with shortly red-shift, meanwhile, a shoulder at 380nm emerged, which assigned to the surface states on the CDs-S generated. The PL spectra (Fig. 4b) showed that the fluorescence intensity increased and shortly red-shift with the temperature increased, which is consistent with the UV–vis absorption. The fluorescence peak at 160°C is attributed to the polymer and the shortly red-shift with the temperature increased may due to the emerged of CDs, thus, it is suggested that the emission is assigned to the composition of nanoparticle size effect and surface states.

Based on the above observations, we propose a formation mechanism of the CDs-S prepared from thioacetic acid, including polymerization, nucleation and growth ( Fig. 5). Initially, thioacetic acid reacts in the high temperature and high pressure through intermolecular dehydration, which leads to the formation of large-sized polymer nanoparticles. During heating, the nucleation began to form with the splitting of polymer nanoparticles, resulting in the coexistence of polymers and CDs-S. With the extension of heating time and increased heating temperature, the number and the size of the CDs-S increase with the disappearance of polymers and only the CDs-S is remained finally.

Due to the S element on the organics, it is necessary to investigate the influence of S element in the synthesis of CDs-S. Herein, another four precursors were treated in the same procedure. Fig. 6 depict the TEM of carbon materials prepared from C2H4O (CDs-O), C2H5NO (CDs-N), C2H5NS (CDs-N,S), C2H4OS (CDs-S). Large-sized polymer fragments were detected in the TEM of CDs (Fig. 6a). The CH3CH2OH, as the other low-molecule-weight organics similar to C2H4O, was use to synthesis the CDs and the result exhibits that the products were large-sized polymer as well ( Fig. 7). It turns to CDs when the carbon sources substituted for those contain N or/and S (Fig. 6b-d). The average sizes of CDs-N, CDs-N, S, CDs-S were 5.4nm, 4.7nm and 6.2nm, respectively. The average diameter of CDs-N,S is lower than others. Thus, it can be deduced that N and/or S states may promote the nucleation of CDs and the CDs-N, S might due to the synergy effect of the doped nitrogen and sulfur atoms. This result is consistent with the PL intensity of CDs-N, S in the following discussion.

It has been reported that the surface states will be more prominent impact on the photophysical properties of CDs [28]. Thus, the precursors with or without N and/or S can cause the different optical phenomenon. UV–Vis spectra ( Fig. 8a) reveal that the surface states of CDs-S exhibit a strong absorbance at 320nm which is the same as other samples (labelled as O-states) while another weak peak can be found at about 430nm which might due to the S atoms doped in the CDs-S (labelled as S-states). The PL intensity of CDs-S is 4.5 fold than CDs-O while CDs-N and CDs-N,S are 2 fold than CDs-O (Fig. 8b). It is suggested that the CDs-N and CDs-N,S have the same PL origin, which is different from that of the CDs-O [23]. In detail, the optical properties of CDs-S is similar to CDs-N,S: (1) Two peaks can be found on the UV/Vis absorption spectrum at 320nm and 430nm which have been discussed before while there are three peaks on the absorption spectrum of CDs-N,S at 320nm, 390nm and 450nm which assigned to O-states, N-states and S-states, respectively; (2) The maximum emission wavelength (λex=360nm) of CDs-S is 460nm, which is about 35nm red-shift the CDs-N,S; (3) the lifetime of the CDs-S is 4.05ns, which is shorter than that of the CDs-N,S. However, the CDs-O exhibit different optical properties with the CDs-N, S ( Fig. 9a): (1) No obvious peak was found in the UV/Vis absorbance of the CDs-O solution besides a broad absorption; (2) the emission wavelength (λex=360nm) of the CDs-O is 420nm, while it is 435nm for CDs-N,S which might due to the N and/or S states on the surface; (3) the lifetime of the CDs-O (3.46ns) is much shorter than that of the N,S-CDs (6.16ns) which are shown in the supporting information ( Fig. 10). In another hand, the optical properties of the CDs-N are similar to the CDs-N, S as well (Fig. 9b): (1) On the UV/Vis absorption spectra, a shoulder at 320nm could be observed and another weak broad peak can be found at about 380nm which might due to the N-states. The N-states can also be found in the absorption spectrum of CDs-N,S at about 390nm; (2) The maximum emission wavelength (λex=360nm) of CDs-N is 435nm, which is similar to the CDs-N,S; (3) the lifetime of the N-CDs is 5.64ns, which is close to that of the N,S-CDs. Thus, the PL origin of CDs-S is different from CDs-N. Based on the above discussion, it can be deduced that the optical properties of CDs-N,S should have the same PL origin with both the CDs-N and the CDs-S, which are different from that of the CDs-O.

Thus, we proposed a model for the mechanism to explain the PL processes of the four CDs. The CDs-O have different kinds of surface states (O-states) which caused a broad UV/Vis absorption band and broad and excitation-dependent emission spectra owing to a relatively wide distribution of different energy levels. The nitrogen-doping and sulfur-doping introduce two new kinds of surface states (N-states and S-states). As the density of the N-state is comparable with that of those O-states in the N-CDs, the PL spectra are still broad and excitation-dependent, even though the PL intensity of the CDs-S is higher than that of the CDs-O.

In summary, we have developed a one step synthesis of CDs-S using thioacetic acid as the carbon and sulfur source simultaneously for the first time. Through changing the reaction temperature, the formation process of CDs-S could be observed by TEM, and it could be further confirmed by UV–Vis absorbance and PL. Four precursors were used to confirm the influence of N and/or S atoms in the synthesis of CDs. It reveals that low-molecule-weight organics can’t be synthesized to CDs at 220°C, while the precursors containing with N and/or S do. Interestingly, the emission spectra of CDs from different precursors were consistent with the N and S states, which might be in connection with the photoluminescence mechanism.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. LR14A040002 and LQ13E020003, the Project of the National Nature Science Foundation of China under Grant No 51402077, 51272243, 61370049 and 61405185.
